agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The described architecture has a synchronous call chain from Order Service → Payment Service → Stripe, creating a fragile critical path where any single failure cascades upstream. The fire-and-forget notification path silently loses emails. This analysis enumerates seven distinct failure modes and prescribes specific resilience patterns — circuit breakers, retries with backoff, idempotency keys, transactional outbox, and a dead-letter queue — to harden each integration point.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Accepts customer orders, orchestrates payment and notification. Owns the order lifecycle and is the system's primary entry point.","boundaries":"Owns Order aggregate. Must not embed payment or notification logic beyond triggering them."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Abstracts payment processing. Translates internal payment requests into Stripe API calls and normalizes responses.","boundaries":"Owns Payment aggregate (payment intent, status, idempotency keys). Encapsulates all Stripe-specific logic behind its own API contract."},{"name":"Stripe (Third-Party Gateway)","responsibility":"External payment processor. Outside our operational control.","boundaries":"Untrusted external dependency. Latency, availability, and response semantics are governed by Stripe's SLA, not ours."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sends confirmation emails and other customer communications.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates and delivery tracking. Should be fully decoupled from order success/failure."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP POST /payments with idempotency key. Returns payment status or structured error.","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral (synchronous)"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Stripe","contract":"Synchronous HTTPS to Stripe API with idempotency key forwarding. Stripe controls latency and error semantics.","coupling_type":"temporal + platform (synchronous to external)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Fire-and-forget HTTP POST /notifications. No response verification, no delivery guarantee.","coupling_type":"fire-and-forget (unacknowledged asynchronous)"}],"data_flow":"1. Client → Order Service: place order. 2. Order Service → Payment Service (sync HTTP): charge customer. 3. Payment Service → Stripe (sync HTTPS): execute charge. 4. Stripe → Payment Service → Order Service: payment result propagates back. 5. Order Service → Notification Service (fire-and-forget): send confirmation email. Each synchronous hop adds its latency to the client-perceived response time. The notification path has no feedback loop.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker","rationale":"Prevents cascade failure when Payment Service or Stripe is degraded. Fails fast instead of accumulating blocked threads and exhausting connection pools."},{"pattern":"Retry with Exponential Backoff and Jitter","rationale":"Handles transient failures (network blips, 503s) on both the Payment Service and Stripe hops without creating thundering-herd retry storms."},{"pattern":"Idempotency Keys","rationale":"Critical for payment operations. Retries and circuit breaker half-open probes must not double-charge customers. Idempotency keys make retries safe."},{"pattern":"Timeouts with Budgets","rationale":"Each synchronous hop needs an explicit timeout. The Order Service should set an overall timeout budget and allocate sub-budgets to downstream calls so one slow dependency cannot block indefinitely."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Replaces fire-and-forget notification with a reliable pattern. Order Service writes a notification event to an outbox table in the same transaction as the order state change. A poller or CDC process relays it to the Notification Service."},{"pattern":"Dead-Letter Queue","rationale":"Captures notification and payment events that fail after all retries. Enables manual inspection and replay without data loss."},{"pattern":"Bulkhead","rationale":"Isolates the Payment Service HTTP client thread pool from other Order Service operations so that payment latency spikes do not starve unrelated order queries."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Ordering","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"core","aggregates":["PaymentIntent","PaymentIdempotencyRecord"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest","DeliveryReceipt"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Ordering","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Ordering dictates when payment is needed; Payment Service conforms to Ordering's request contract."},{"upstream":"Payment","downstream":"Stripe (External)","relationship":"Conformist with Anti-Corruption Layer — Payment Service conforms to Stripe's API but wraps it in an ACL to isolate Stripe-specific models."},{"upstream":"Ordering","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via Transactional Outbox — Ordering publishes domain events; Notification reacts asynchronously."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Keep Order→Payment synchronous rather than moving to async saga","gains":"Simpler programming model; client gets immediate payment confirmation; no saga state machine to manage","sacrifices":"Order Service availability is coupled to Payment Service and Stripe availability; higher tail latency under degraded conditions","alternatives_considered":["Async saga with compensation (higher complexity, better availability decoupling)","Reservation pattern with deferred capture (good for high-value orders but adds a second API call)"]},{"decision":"Replace fire-and-forget with Transactional Outbox for notifications","gains":"Guaranteed at-least-once delivery of notification events; no silent email loss; audit trail","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure (outbox table, poller or CDC); slight increase in order-write latency due to extra row insert; must handle duplicate delivery in Notification Service","alternatives_considered":["Message broker (RabbitMQ/Kafka) directly — reliable but introduces a new infrastructure dependency and doesn't solve the dual-write problem without outbox anyway","Keep fire-and-forget and accept occasional email loss — unacceptable for order confirmations"]},{"decision":"Circuit breaker on Payment Service call","gains":"Fail-fast behavior protects Order Service from thread starvation; gives Payment Service time to recover","sacrifices":"Orders submitted during open-circuit window will fail immediately; requires clear UX for 'try again later'","alternatives_considered":["Queue-and-retry on the Order side (delays customer response, adds state complexity)"]},{"decision":"Bulkhead isolation for payment HTTP client pool","gains":"Payment degradation cannot consume all Order Service threads; read-path queries remain responsive","sacrifices":"Reduced peak throughput for payments specifically (pool is capped); tuning pool size requires load testing","alternatives_considered":["Single shared pool with high limits (simpler but no isolation)"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment gateway","evolution_path":"The ACL in Payment Service means Stripe can be swapped for Adyen, Braintree, or a multi-gateway strategy without changing Order Service or the Payment Service API contract."},{"area":"Notification delivery","evolution_path":"Transactional outbox decouples notification triggering from delivery. The Notification Service can evolve to support SMS, push, webhooks, or multi-channel without changing Order Service."},{"area":"Sync-to-async migration for payments","evolution_path":"If scale demands it, the Order→Payment integration can evolve to an async saga. The idempotency keys and outbox infrastructure lay the groundwork — the idempotency contract is already retry-safe."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Synchronous Order→Payment→Stripe call chain","reason":"Intentionally rigid. Payment confirmation before order completion is a business invariant. This coupling is the cost of immediate consistency. Mitigated by circuit breaker and timeouts, but the synchronous nature is load-bearing."},{"area":"Idempotency key contract","reason":"Once deployed, the idempotency key format and semantics become a cross-service contract. Changing it requires coordinated deployment across Order Service and Payment Service."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Stripe outage cascades to order placement failure","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Circuit breaker on Payment Service→Stripe call. When open, Payment Service returns a structured 'payment unavailable' error. Order Service can either fail the order with a clear message or (if business allows) place the order in a 'pending payment' state for deferred retry."},{"risk":"Double-charge due to retry without idempotency","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Enforce idempotency keys at every layer: Order Service generates a key per order attempt, Payment Service forwards it to Stripe. Payment Service stores idempotency records to short-circuit duplicate requests even if Stripe is unreachable."},{"risk":"Silent notification loss (current fire-and-forget design)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Replace with transactional outbox pattern. Notification event is written atomically with order state. Poller/CDC relays to Notification Service with retries. Failed notifications land in a dead-letter queue for manual inspection."},{"risk":"Thread/connection pool exhaustion in Order Service due to slow Payment Service","severity":"high","mitigation":"Bulkhead pattern: dedicated HTTP client pool for payment calls, sized independently. Combined with aggressive timeouts (e.g., 5s connect, 10s read) and circuit breaker."},{"risk":"Partial failure: payment succeeds at Stripe but response lost on the way back","severity":"high","mitigation":"Idempotency key allows safe retry — Stripe returns the original result. Payment Service should also implement a reconciliation job that polls Stripe for recent charges and reconciles against local payment records."},{"risk":"Timeout misconfiguration creating cascading slowness","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Implement a timeout budget: Order Service sets a total budget (e.g., 15s), allocates sub-budgets to each downstream call. If payment consumes 12s, notification gets at most 3s (though with outbox this becomes moot for notifications). Monitor p99 latency and alert on budget violations."},{"risk":"Outbox poller lag causing delayed notifications","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Monitor outbox table depth. Use CDC (e.g., Debezium) instead of polling for near-real-time relay. Set SLA alerting on notification delivery latency."},{"risk":"Dead-letter queue grows unmonitored","severity":"low","mitigation":"Alert on DLQ depth. Provide an admin interface for inspecting and replaying failed events. Establish an operational runbook."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (immediate): Add explicit timeouts to all HTTP calls — Order→Payment (10s), Payment→Stripe (8s). Add idempotency key generation in Order Service and forwarding through Payment Service to Stripe. Phase 2 (next sprint): Implement circuit breaker on both hops (Order→Payment, Payment→Stripe) using a proven library (e.g., Resilience4j, Polly, or Hystrix-successor). Add bulkhead isolation for the payment HTTP client pool. Phase 3 (following sprint): Replace fire-and-forget notification with transactional outbox. Add outbox table to Order Service database, implement poller/CDC relay, add dead-letter queue. Phase 4 (hardening): Add Stripe reconciliation job. Add monitoring dashboards for circuit breaker state, outbox depth, DLQ depth, and per-hop latency percentiles.","files_to_create_or_modify":["order-service/src/clients/PaymentClient — add timeout, retry with backoff, circuit breaker, bulkhead, idempotency key header","order-service/src/domain/Order — generate idempotency key on order creation, store it on the aggregate","order-service/src/infrastructure/OutboxTable — schema and repository for transactional outbox","order-service/src/infrastructure/OutboxPoller — polls outbox, relays to Notification Service or message broker","payment-service/src/clients/StripeClient — add timeout, retry with backoff, circuit breaker, idempotency key forwarding","payment-service/src/domain/PaymentIdempotencyRecord — deduplication store for payment requests","payment-service/src/jobs/StripeReconciliation — periodic job to reconcile local records with Stripe","notification-service/src/handlers/NotificationHandler — add idempotent processing (dedup by notification ID)","infrastructure/dead-letter-queue — DLQ configuration for failed outbox events","monitoring/dashboards — circuit breaker state, outbox depth, DLQ depth, p99 latency per hop"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the Resilience4j (Java) or Polly (.NET) or equivalent library for circuit breaker and retry — do not hand-roll state machines. Idempotency keys should be UUIDv4, generated once per order attempt and stored on the Order aggregate. Outbox table schema: (id, aggregate_type, aggregate_id, event_type, payload, created_at, published_at). Poller should use SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for concurrent-safe polling.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT retry payment calls without idempotency keys — this is the #1 path to double-charging","Do NOT set circuit breaker thresholds too aggressively — a single timeout should not open the circuit; use a sliding window (e.g., 50% failure rate over 10 calls)","Do NOT use the outbox poller as the sole delivery mechanism without monitoring — unmonitored outbox tables silently accumulate stale events","Do NOT share a single HTTP connection pool across payment and non-payment calls — this defeats bulkhead isolation","Do NOT catch and swallow exceptions from Payment Service — propagate structured errors so Order Service can make informed decisions (fail order vs. defer vs. retry)","Do NOT assume Stripe's idempotency window is infinite — Stripe keys expire after 24h; reconciliation job covers the gap"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Failure mode enumeration — these are well-understood distributed systems failure modes","Resilience patterns — circuit breaker, retry, idempotency, outbox are industry-standard","Risk severity ranking — double-charge and cascade failures are objectively critical"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact timeout values — depend on measured latency percentiles which are not provided","Whether business rules permit a 'pending payment' order state — requires domain authority input","Infrastructure choices for outbox relay (polling vs CDC) — depends on existing infrastructure stack"],"assumptions":["Order placement requires synchronous payment confirmation (business invariant)","Email confirmation is important enough to warrant guaranteed delivery but does not need to be synchronous","The team has access to a circuit breaker library and is not constrained to hand-rolled solutions","Database supports SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED or equivalent for outbox polling"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"The described architecture has a synchronous call chain from Order Service → Payment Service → Stripe, creating a fragile critical path where any single failure cascades upstream. The fire-and-forget notification path silently loses emails. This analysis enumerates seven distinct failure modes and prescribes specific resilience patterns — circuit breakers, retries with backoff, idempotency keys, transactional outbox, and a dead-letter queue — to harden each integration poin

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this architecture's failure modes and design resilience patterns.

Classification: `none` — sufficient constraints exist to perform architectural analysis. The architecture is clearly described with specific integration patterns.

Notification reacts asynchronously."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Keep Order→Payment synchronous rather than moving to async saga","gains":"Simpler programming model; client gets immediate payment confirmation; no saga state machine to manage","sacrifices":"Order Service availability is coupled to Payment Service and Stripe availability; higher tail latency under degraded conditions","alternatives_considered":["Async saga with compensation (higher complexity, better availability decoupling)","Reservation pattern with deferred capture (good for high-value orders but adds a second API call)"]},{"decision":"Replace fire-and-forget with Transactional Outbox for notifications","gains":"Guaranteed at-least-once delivery of notification events; no silent email loss; audit trail","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure (outbox table, poller or CDC); slight increase in order-write latency due to extra row insert; must handle duplicate delivery in Notification Service","alternatives_considered":["Message broker (RabbitMQ/Kafka) directly — reliable but introduces a new infrastructure dependency and doesn't solve the dual-write problem without outbox anyway","Keep fire-and-forget and accept occasional email loss — unacceptable for order confirmations"]},{"decision":"Circuit breaker on Payment Service call","gains":"Fail-fast behavior protects Order Service from thread starvation; gives Payment Service time to recover","sacrifices":"Orders submitted during open-circuit window will fail immediately; requires clear UX for 'try again later'","alternatives_considered":["Queue-and-retry on the Order side (delays customer response, adds state complexity)"]},{"decision":"Bulkhead isolation for payment HTTP client pool","gains":"Payment degradation cannot consume all Order Service threads; read-path queries remain responsive","sacrifices":"Reduced peak throughput for payments specifically (pool is capped); tuning pool size requires load testing","alternatives_considered":["Single shared pool with high limits (simpler but no isolation)"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment gateway","evolution_path":"The ACL in Payment Service means Stripe can be swapped for Adyen, Braintree, or a multi-gateway strategy without changing Order Service or the Payment Service API contract."},{"area":"Notification delivery","evolution_path":"Transactional outbox decouples notification triggering from delivery. The Notification Service can evolve to support SMS, push, webhooks, or multi-channel without changing Order Service."},{"area":"Sync-to-async migration for payments","evolution_path":"If scale demands it, the Order→Payment integration can evolve to an async saga. The idempotency keys and outbox infrastructure lay the groundwork — the idempotency contract is already retry-safe."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Synchronous Order→Payment→Stripe call chain","reason":"Intentionally rigid. Payment confirmation before order completion is a business invariant. This coupling is the cost of immediate consistency. Mitigated by circuit breaker and timeouts, but the synchronous nature is load-bearing."},{"area":"Idempotency key contract","reason":"Once deployed, the idempotency key format and semantics become a cross-service contract. Changing it requires coordinated deployment across Order Service and Payment Service."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Stripe outage cascades to order placement failure","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Circuit breaker on Payment Service→Stripe call. When open, Payment Service returns a structured 'payment unavailable' error. Order Service can either fail the order with a clear message or (if business allows) place the order in a 'pending payment' state for deferred retry."},{"risk":"Double-charge due to retry without idempotency","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Enforce idempotency keys at every layer: Order Service generates a key per order attempt, Payment Service forwards it to Stripe. Payment Service stores idempotency records to short-circuit duplicate requests even if Stripe is unreachable."},{"risk":"Silent notification loss (current fire-and-forget design)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Replace with transactional outbox pattern. Notification event is written atomically with order state. Poller/CDC relays to Notification Service with retries. Failed notifications land in a dead-letter queue for manual inspection."},{"risk":"Thread/connection pool exhaustion in Order Service due to slow Payment Service","severity":"high","mitigation":"Bulkhead pattern: dedicated HTTP client pool for payment calls, sized independently. Combined with aggressive timeouts (e.g., 5s connect, 10s read) and circuit breaker."},{"risk":"Partial failure: payment succeeds at Stripe but response lost on the way back","severity":"high","mitigation":"Idempotency key allows safe retry — Stripe returns the original result. Payment Service should also implement a reconciliation job that polls Stripe for recent charges and reconciles against local payment records."},{"risk":"Timeout misconfiguration creating cascading slowness","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Implement a timeout budget: Order Service sets a total budget (e.g., 15s), allocates sub-budgets to each downstream call. Establish an operational runbook."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (immediate): Add explicit timeouts to all HTTP calls — Order→Payment (10s), Payment→Stripe (8s). Phase 2 (next sprint): Implement circuit breaker on both hops (Order→Payment, Payment→Stripe) using a proven library (e.g., Resilience4j, Polly, or Hystrix-successor). Add bulkhead isolation for the payment HTTP client pool. Phase 3 (following sprint): Replace fire-and-forget notification with transactional outbox. Add outbox table to Order Service database, implement poller/CDC relay, add dead-letter queue. Phase 4 (hardening): Add Stripe reconciliation job. Add monitoring dashboards for circuit breaker state, outbox depth, DLQ depth, and per-hop latency percentiles.","files_to_create_or_modify":["order-service/src/clients/PaymentClient — add timeout, retry with backoff, circuit breaker, bulkhead, idempotency key header","order-service/src/domain/Order — generate idempotency key on order creation, store it on the aggregate","order-service/src/infrastructure/OutboxTable — schema and repository for transactional outbox","order-service/src/infrastructure/OutboxPoller — polls outbox, relays to Notification Service or message broker","payment-service/src/clients/StripeClient — add timeout, retry with backoff, circuit breaker, idempotency key forwarding","payment-service/src/domain/PaymentIdempotencyRecord — deduplication store for payment requests","payment-service/src/jobs/StripeReconciliation — periodic job to reconcile local records with Stripe","notification-service/src/handlers/NotificationHandler — add idempotent processing (dedup by notification ID)","infrastructure/dead-letter-queue — DLQ configuration for failed outbox events","monitoring/dashboards — circuit breaker state, outbox depth, DLQ depth, p99 latency per hop"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the Resilience4j (Java) or Polly (.NET) or equivalent library for circuit breaker and retry — do not hand-roll state machines. Idempotency keys should be UUIDv4, generated once per order attempt and stored on the Order aggregate. Outbox table schema: (id, aggregate_type, aggregate_id, event_type, payload, created_at, published_at). Poller should use SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for concurrent-safe polling.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT retry payment calls without idempotency keys — this is the #1 path to double-charging","Do NOT set circuit breaker thresholds too aggressively — a single timeout should not open the circuit; use a sliding window (e.g., 50% failure rate over 10 calls)","Do NOT use the outbox poller as the sole delivery mechanism without monitoring — unmonitored outbox tables silently accumulate stale events","Do NOT share a single HTTP connection pool across payment and non-payment calls — this defeats bulkhead isolation","Do NOT catch and swallow exceptions from Payment Service — propagate structured errors so Order Service can make informed decisions (fail order vs. defer vs. retry)","Do NOT assume Stripe's idempotency window is infinite — Stripe keys expire after 24h; reconciliation job covers the gap"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Failure mode enumeration — these are well-understood distributed systems failure modes","Resilience patterns — circuit breaker, retry, idempotency, outbox are industry-standard","Risk severity ranking — double-charge and cascade failures are objectively critical"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact timeout values — depend on measured latency percentiles which are not provided","Whether business rules permit a 'pending payment' order state — requires domain authority input","Infrastructure choices for outbox relay (polling vs CDC) — depends on existing infrastructure stack"],"assumptions":["Order placement requires synchronous payment confirmation (business invariant)","Email confirmation is important enough to warrant guaranteed delivery but does not need to be synchronous","The team has access to a circuit breaker library and is not constrained to hand-rolled solutions","Database supports SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED or equivalent for outbox polling"]}}
